questions.stem: A 65-year-old man presents with acute central chest pain radiating to the left arm, associated with breathlessness and sweating. On examination, heart rate 105 bpm, blood pressure 80/50 mmHg, JVP elevated, and bibasal crackles. ECG shows ST elevation in leads V1–V4. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute anterior STEMI with cardiogenic shock
questions.explanation: The combination of ischaemic chest pain, ST elevation on ECG, hypotension, and pulmonary oedema is classic for cardiogenic shock due to acute STEMI. Massive PE typically presents with right heart strain on ECG and a different pattern of shock. Tamponade would show pulsus paradoxus and electrical alternans. Critical aortic stenosis has a characteristic murmur. Septic shock would have fever and infection source.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman with a history of hypertension and diabetes presents with progressive dyspnoea over two days. She has no chest pain. On examination, heart rate 100 bpm, blood pressure 90/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 24, oxygen saturations 88% on room air, and bilateral crackles. ECG shows Q waves in leads II, III, aVF. Troponin I is elevated at 5 ng/mL (normal <0.04). What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute anaemia due to gastrointestinal bleed
Exacerbation of COPD with cor pulmonale
Silent inferior myocardial infarction with cardiogenic shock
questions.explanation: Silent MI is common in elderly diabetics; the ECG shows inferior Q waves and elevated troponin, with hypotension and pulmonary oedema indicating cardiogenic shock. PE would likely show sinus tachycardia and RV strain on ECG. COPD exacerbation would have hyperinflation and wheeze. Anaemia would have pallor and low Hb. Sepsis would have fever and a source.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man presents two days after an anterior ST-elevation myocardial infarction treated with primary PCI. He now has sudden onset breathlessness and hypotension. On examination, heart rate 110 bpm, blood pressure 85/50 mmHg, JVP raised, and a harsh pansystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border with a palpable thrill. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Free wall cardiac rupture with tamponade
Papillary muscle rupture with acute mitral regurgitation
questions.explanation: Post-MI VSR presents with a new harsh pansystolic murmur, thrill, and cardiogenic shock. Papillary muscle rupture causes a soft mitral regurgitation murmur radiating to the axilla but no thrill. Free wall rupture leads to tamponade with electrical alternans and pulsus paradoxus. Recurrent infarction would have new ST changes. PE would not have a murmur.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with sudden onset of severe dyspnoea and chest tightness following an emotional argument. She has no cardiovascular risk factors. On examination, heart rate 115 bpm, blood pressure 85/55 mmHg, and bilateral crackles. ECG shows deep T-wave inversions in leads V3–V6. Troponin I is mildly elevated. Coronary angiography shows no significant stenosis. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute coronary syndrome due to coronary vasospasm
questions.explanation: Takotsubo (stress) cardiomyopathy is triggered by emotional stress, presents with chest pain, pulmonary oedema, and shock, with T-wave inversions and mild troponin rise; angiography is normal. Myocarditis often has viral prodrome and more global dysfunction. Coronary vasospasm would show transient ST elevation. PE would have RV strain on echo. Peripartum cardiomyopathy occurs around pregnancy.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with known ischaemic cardiomyopathy (LVEF 25%) on optimal medical therapy presents with worsening breathlessness and fatigue over three days. He has been non-compliant with his diuretics. On examination, heart rate 95 bpm, blood pressure 88/55 mmHg, JVP elevated, and crackles up to the lung apices. There is no fever or chest pain. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Septic shock from lower respiratory tract infection
Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
Acute-on-chronic heart failure with cardiogenic shock
Acute renal failure causing fluid overload
questions.explanation: In a patient with known low LVEF, decompensation due to non-adherence leads to low output and congestion, i.e., cardiogenic shock. Sepsis would involve fever and raised inflammatory markers. PE would have acute onset and RV strain. Atrial fibrillation typically causes tachycardia and irregular pulse. Renal failure would have oliguria and electrolyte abnormalities.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man presents with chest pain and breathlessness. Blood pressure 85/50 mmHg, heart rate 110 bpm, JVP elevated, cold peripheries. ECG shows ST elevation in anterior leads. What is the most appropriate test to confirm the diagnosis of cardiogenic shock?
questions.explanation: The ECG is diagnostic of STEMI causing cardiogenic shock. Troponin confirms myocardial injury but not the shock mechanism. Echocardiogram assesses function but is not the first diagnostic test for STEMI.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with known heart failure presents with worsening dyspnoea and hypotension. Blood pressure 90/60 mmHg, heart rate 100 bpm, bibasal crackles, peripheral oedema. What is the single next best investigation to assess the cause of her likely cardiogenic shock?
questions.explanation: Echocardiogram is the first-line test to assess left ventricular function, valvular disease, and exclude mechanical complications. Pulmonary artery catheter is invasive and not routinely needed; BNP is supportive but not diagnostic.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man, 2 days post-inferior STEMI, develops sudden hypotension and a new pansystolic murmur. Suspecting a mechanical complication, which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Echocardiogram with Doppler can directly visualise a ventricular septal rupture or papillary muscle rupture and demonstrate shunt flow. Right heart catheterisation can confirm step-up but is invasive and not first line.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with no cardiac history presents with sudden-onset pleuritic chest pain, haemoptysis, and hypotension. Blood pressure 80/50 mmHg, heart rate 120 bpm, oxygen saturation 88% on air, JVP elevated. ECG shows sinus tachycardia. Which investigation is most appropriate to establish the cause of shock?
questions.explanation: CT pulmonary angiogram is the gold standard to diagnose pulmonary embolism, the likely cause of obstructive shock. Echocardiogram may show right heart strain but does not confirm PE. D-dimer is non-specific and not diagnostic in high probability.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man with severe aortic stenosis presents with syncope and hypotension. Blood pressure 85/50 mmHg, heart rate 105 bpm, a late-peaking systolic murmur at the right upper sternal border. What is the single best investigation to confirm the diagnosis of cardiogenic shock due to aortic stenosis?
questions.explanation: Transthoracic echocardiogram is the initial test to assess valve area and gradient, confirming severe aortic stenosis and its contribution to shock. Cardiac catheterisation is reserved when non-invasive imaging is inconclusive or for coronary assessment.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with acute anterior ST-elevation myocardial infarction 2 hours after symptom onset. Blood pressure is 85/50 mmHg, heart rate 110 bpm, and crackles are heard up to the mid-zones. What is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
questions.explanation: In cardiogenic shock due to STEMI, immediate revascularization with primary PCI is the first-line definitive therapy, as it improves outcomes compared to thrombolysis or inotropes alone.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman with severe aortic stenosis (aortic valve area 0.6 cm²) presents with syncope, hypotension (80/45 mmHg), and pulmonary oedema. What is the most appropriate definitive therapy?
questions.explanation: In cardiogenic shock due to severe aortic stenosis, balloon aortic valvuloplasty provides rapid haemodynamic improvement as a bridge to definitive valve replacement; it is preferred acutely over surgery or TAVI in many centres.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old previously healthy woman presents with acute myocarditis, left ventricular ejection fraction 20%, and cardiogenic shock refractory to inotropes. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
questions.explanation: In fulminant myocarditis with refractory shock, VA-ECMO provides biventricular circulatory support as a bridge to recovery, which is the definitive therapy; immunosuppression is not routinely indicated.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with an inferior STEMI 3 days ago develops sudden pulmonary oedema and hypotension. Examination reveals a new loud holosystolic murmur at the apex. What is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
options: Emergency mitral valve repair or replacement
questions.explanation: Acute papillary muscle rupture causing severe mitral regurgitation and cardiogenic shock requires urgent surgical correction (repair or replacement); medical therapy alone is insufficient.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman, 2 weeks postpartum, presents with dyspnoea, hypotension, and left ventricular ejection fraction 20%. She is diagnosed with peripartum cardiomyopathy causing cardiogenic shock. What is the most appropriate definitive therapy?
questions.explanation: In peripartum cardiomyopathy with cardiogenic shock, bromocriptine is recommended as specific therapy (alongside standard heart failure treatment) as it suppresses prolactin and improves outcomes; inotropes and mechanical support are adjunctive.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with crushing central chest pain for 1 hour. ECG shows anterior ST-elevation. BP 84/52 mmHg, HR 110 bpm, RR 22, SpO2 94% on 15L O2. He has crackles to mid-zones. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: Primary PCI is the definitive treatment for STEMI with cardiogenic shock; delaying reperfusion for diuretics or inotropes is not recommended. Fluids risk worsening pulmonary oedema, and NIV does not address the underlying ischaemia.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man, day 3 after inferior STEMI, develops acute dyspnoea and hypotension (BP 82/50 mmHg). He has a loud pansystolic murmur at apex, crackles, and cool peripheries. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: Suspect acute mitral regurgitation due to papillary muscle rupture; echo confirms diagnosis and guides urgent surgery. GTN and diuretics can worsen hypotension, and IABP or surgery without imaging is premature.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman presents with inferior STEMI, BP 88/54 mmHg, HR 45 bpm (sinus bradycardia). JVP is elevated but lung fields are clear. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: Right ventricular infarction causes preload-dependent shock; fluids are first-line if lungs clear. Atropine may help bradycardia but fluids are more urgent; PCI is definitive but not immediate before stabilisation.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old woman with known ischaemic cardiomyopathy (LVEF 30%) on lisinopril, bisoprolol, and furosemide presents with hypotension (BP 86/52 mmHg), oliguria, cool peripheries, JVP 10 cm, and crackles at bases. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: This is cardiogenic shock with congestion; dobutamine improves cardiac output. Diuretics may worsen hypotension, fluids are contraindicated, and echo can wait. Holding ACEi/BB is important but not the immediate next step.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old man with severe aortic stenosis (AVA 0.7 cm²) awaiting TAVI becomes hypotensive (BP 78/44 mmHg) and dyspnoeic after a fall. He has a systolic murmur, cool extremities, and JVP elevated. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: Urgent echo is needed to assess if shock is due to critical AS or another cause (e.g., tamponade, MI). Fluids or diuretics may be harmful without diagnosis; dobutamine is risky in severe AS; valvuloplasty requires echo confirmation.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man presents with acute anterior STEMI. Four hours after thrombolysis he remains hypotensive (BP 90/60 mmHg), heart rate 110 bpm, and has cold peripheries. Which single laboratory finding most strongly indicates that he has developed cardiogenic shock requiring immediate escalation?
questions.explanation: A serum lactate >4 mmol/L is a NICE threshold for tissue hypoperfusion in cardiogenic shock, indicating severity beyond early compensation and warranting urgent escalation. Lactate 3.2 is below this threshold, and the other markers are less specific for shock severity.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman with chronic heart failure (LVEF 30%) on optimal medical therapy is admitted with dyspnoea and BP 100/65 mmHg. She is alert and comfortable at rest, with warm peripheries. Which single clinical parameter, if present, would prompt urgent critical care review?
options: Urine output less than 0.5 mL/kg/hour over 4 hours
questions.explanation: Oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/h for >2 hours) is a red-flag criterion for acute kidney injury and indicates renal hypoperfusion from worsening cardiac output, despite seemingly stable vital signs, requiring escalation to critical care. The other parameters are only mildly abnormal and not immediate thresholds.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a large inferior ST-elevation myocardial infarction develops hypotension, raised jugular venous pressure, and oliguria. On auscultation of the chest, what finding would most strongly suggest isolated right ventricular infarction rather than biventricular failure?
questions.explanation: Clear lung fields (absence of crackles) in the setting of elevated JVP and hypotension is classic for right ventricular infarction, as it rules out left ventricular failure and pulmonary congestion, guiding fluid resuscitation rather than diuretics.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man in cardiogenic shock following anterior MI has a new pansystolic murmur. Which clinical feature would suggest acute mitral regurgitation rather than ventricular septal rupture as the cause?
options: There is a palpable thrill at the left parasternal area
The JVP is elevated with prominent v waves
The murmur is loudest at the left lower sternal edge
The murmur is accompanied by a mid-systolic click
The murmur radiates to the axilla
questions.explanation: Mitral regurgitation murmur radiates to the axilla, whereas VSD murmur is loudest at the left lower sternal edge often with a thrill. The other options are not reliably differentiating; a mid-systolic click suggests MVP but not acute MR.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old woman with known aortic stenosis (valve area 0.7 cm²) presents with syncope and hypotension (BP 85/40 mmHg). Which cardiovascular examination finding, if present, would be most consistent with critical aortic stenosis as the cause of her shock?
questions.explanation: Critical aortic stenosis results in a low stroke volume and thus a narrow pulse pressure. Bounding pulse occurs in conditions like aortic regurgitation, wide pulse pressure in AR or sepsis, pulsus paradoxus in cardiac tamponade, and pulsus alternans in severe LV dysfunction.
